answer:
**Charles Comte's foundational claim** appears in his early chapters establishing *liberty* (the absence of slavery's obstacles to human faculties) as the precondition for *property*, which manifests as the free occupation and appropriation of unowned things to satisfy needs.[^1] He argues that without liberty, no genuine property (as the fruit of labor) can form, since slavery negates rights and duties essential to human development; property emerges when free individuals first seize res nullius ("things belonging to no one") with intent to appropriate, as this obeys "a law of their nature" maximizing utility without harm.[^2]

**A representative procedural rule from Yajñavalkya's Book 2** (on *vyavahāra*, legal procedure/disputes) prioritizes *title-deeds* over mere possession in property claims, except where long generational possession (20 years for land) establishes ownership via acquiescence; absent proof, the king enforces via fines or restoration.[^3] For boundary disputes specifically (a common property conflict), neighbors, elders, cowherds, and forest-roamers determine limits via marks (e.g., mounds, trees, anthills), with the king fining false claims or mark-destruction (lowest/highest amercements).[^4]

**Does this *smṛti* procedure presuppose Comtean liberty?** Minimally, yes: it assumes individuals hold disputable *property* (*dhana*, land/*kṣetra*) as a baseline, with rights to sue/prove title via documents/witnesses/or deals—echoing Comte's protection of fruits of free labor from arbitrary seizure.[^5] Free testimony, neighbor-verification, and kingly enforcement imply no total servitude, as parties exercise faculties (speech, proof) without master's whim blocking access to justice. However, Comte would likely deem it *un*-libertarian: hierarchical (*varṇa*-biased witnesses/fines), ordeal-dependent (divine/fire trials for the unproven), and king-centric (arbitrary timelines, royal fines as "property" of state), resembling "political slavery" where authority trumps individual faculties.[^6] Liberty for Comte demands symmetric protection sans caste/kingly caprice; here, procedure *secures* property but under servitude-like constraints.

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, ch.3 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-9b6829).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, ch.4 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/04-de-loccupation-des-choses/translation/#p-8094b8); cf. ch.5 on labor-derived properties needing liberty to exist.

[^3]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, ch.2 [paragraph](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-e9236b) (Yj_2.27); see also Yj_2.24-25 on limitation periods excluding boundaries/king's land.

[^4]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, ch.2 [paragraph](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-382005) (Yj_2.150-153); fines at Yj_2.155.

[^5]: Aligns with Comte ch.3's liberty as "no obstacle...to the innocent exercise of his faculties," enabling property defense.

[^6]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, ch.3 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-86c544) (slavery's "means" like passes/associations banned under liberty).
